original_abstract: This article explores the historical development of the Balling formula, specifically examining the derivation of alcohol factors and attenuation quotients as presented in Balling’s core texts from 1846 and 1854. The study introduces a modified Balling formula that enables the direct calculation of original extract from apparent extract and alcohol levels, bypassing the traditional reliance on Tabarie’s formula. Furthermore, the attenuation quotient ($q$) is identified as an increasingly relevant metric for the expanding craft brewing sector. The enduring value of the Balling formula lies in its ability to characterize the relationship between fermented and residual extracts through a single measurement—a procedure that has remained remarkably stable for over 150 years. Finally, the author proposes that both real extract and alcohol content should be explicitly disclosed to consumers to facilitate a more accurate assessment of a beverage's nutritional profile.
humanized_abstract: Originally intended as an update to an article that had appeared on Bragdon’s Brewing Blog a couple of years ago this article delves more deeply into the historical development of the Balling formula, with particular regard to Balling’s calculation of alcohol factors and attenuation quotients as described in the texts he published in 1846 and 1854. An alternative version of the Balling formula is derived that can be used to calculate original extract from apparent extract and alcohol readings, obviating the need to employ Tabarie’s formula. The attenuation quotient (q) is also determined to be an important parameter, particularly for the craft brewers that are rapidly expanding their sector. The article also alludes to the ongoing value of the Balling formula, which, 155+ years after it was introduced, still allows a fermanted product’s original and post-fermentation extract to be calculated using a single measurement. An interesting and potentially significant ancillary observation is made regarding consumer information, which points to the value of providing a declaration of the real extract and alcohol content of the beverage, in order to allow consumers to get an accurate measure of the nutritional content of their drink.
